summary.overall_assessment: Python and TypeScript overlap on names, but memory and eval are notably split-brain; TypeScript owns richer context/governance orchestration while Python retains separate runtime implementations.

evidence: runtime/memory_store.py exposes tier-aware internals and CMMS pytest subset passed
No Auto/Micro/Ship or tier-routing signals were found in src/state/memory-store.ts

comparisons.reason: Python has explicit CMMS tiering while the TypeScript memory store is still a single-store encrypted SQLite design with no tier model.

evidence: TS context modules include freshness scoring, decay detection, checkpointing, reconstruction boundaries, and durability routing

comparisons.reason: Both runtimes implement context concepts, but TypeScript contains the richer freshness/reconstruction orchestration while Python looks more like a bounded packet/decay engine.

evidence: Smoke checks exported a trajectory JSONL and detected a regression
src/verification contains proof-gate, claim-judge, test-intent-lock, and compliance-governor
No eval-gate/trajectory/regression counterpart was found under src/verification

comparisons.reason: Python owns explicit eval history and regression logic; TypeScript verification focuses on proof/compliance primitives rather than an eval pipeline.

evidence: TS governance stack covers graph validation, collusion checks, lane policy enforcement, and bash command classification

comparisons.reason: Both runtimes have real governance modules, but responsibilities are split differently: Python handles release/tool-plan gates while TypeScript emphasizes graph/lane enforcement and command classification.
